core_identity.title: Exploring Nets, Surface Area & Volume of 3D Solids
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: nets, surface area and volume of 3D solids
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Use nets to connect 3D solids to 2D faces, and calculate surface area and volume accurately by identifying faces, dimensions and consistent methods.

verse_ality.driving_question: What is the relationship between a 3D shape and its 2D net, and how can nets help us understand surface area and volume?

knowing_world.focus: Explore how 2D nets fold into 3D solids, and use this link to reason about surface area and how building a solid relates to volume.
key_concepts: Surface area as total area of all faces
Volume as space inside a solid
Comparing solids (e.g., cube vs triangular prism)

human_story.focus: Connect nets, surface area and volume to human building and design—from pyramids and architecture to modern packaging—where geometry supports efficiency, strength and cost.

expressive_self.focus: Use the net-building tool creatively, noticing how different nets ‘feel’ to fold, which solids are easier/harder to visualise, and how visual surface area supports understanding.
activities: Build your own solids and describe which nets were easiest or most challenging to mentally fold.
Reflect on how seeing faces laid out in a net changes the way you think about surface area.

designed_world.focus: Experiment with building solids (e.g., cube, triangular prism), compare surface areas and volumes, and look for patterns that designers/engineers could use.
design_challenges: Build a cube, then build a triangular prism; compare surface area and volume.
Change dimensions and record what happens to surface area vs volume.
Explain how this tool could help a designer or engineer make better decisions (materials, packaging, efficiency).

regulation_supports: Pause to sketch or trace your mental image of the solid you built, then compare it to the net.
Notice how your hands and eyes support visualising the net folding into a 3D form.

pathways.name: Final Task: Build, Record, Explain
pathways.focus: Use the interactive tool to build at least three different solids, record faces/surface area/volume, and explain a consistent method for calculating each measure.
products: Three different solids built in the tool (screenshots or sketches).
For each solid: number of faces, total surface area, and volume recorded.
A written or spoken explanation of a consistent method used to calculate surface area and volume.
Optional comparison: what patterns did you notice across shapes?

required: Area of rectangles and triangles (and other faces used)
Basic volume knowledge for prisms/cuboids (or readiness to learn from the tool)
Accurate use of units (cm², m², cm³, m³)

builds_toward: Surface area of prisms and cylinders (including curved surfaces)
Volume of prisms and composite solids
Scale factors for area and volume
